content.text:
[cron:2ca3531d-d7c2-4846-b91f-804a1e726f81 Note-taking compliance audit (10-day session review)] ## Task: Note-taking compliance audit — 10-day session review

You are running an isolated audit. Your ONLY job:

1. Using the session-logs skill (read ~/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/session-logs/SKILL.md first, use jq commands), find ALL session JSONL files modified in the last 10 days (2026-03-17 through 2026-03-27).

2. Search each session for any instance when Tiny asked you to take notes, make a note, remember something, or similar directive. Search for these patterns:
   - "take notes"
   - "make a note"
   - "I want you to remember"
   - "note this"
   - "remember this"
   - "don't forget"
   - Any explicit instruction to save something to a file

3. For each finding, record:
   - Date/time of the instruction
   - What Tiny asked you to note
   - Whether you actually noted it (yes/no/partial)
   - If yes: which file it was saved to
   - If no: why not (if discernible from context)

4. Compile findings into a report at `memory/2026-03-27-note-compliance-audit.md` with:
   - Summary stats (total instructions found, total complied, total missed)
   - List of complied notes (with file locations)
   - List of missed notes (with what was asked)
   - Root cause analysis if patterns emerge

5. ALSO: Using the same session files, find any feedback Tiny gave you that was NOT captured in any skills folder or memory file. This includes:
   - Corrections
   - Preferences stated
   - Style critiques
   - Rules he gave you

6. Save any uncaptured feedback to `memory/2026-03-27-uncaptured-feedback.md`

Do NOT draft content. Do NOT do any Nava work. Only audit, record, and report.

When done, output a one-line summary: "AUDIT COMPLETE: X note instructions found, Y complied, Z missed. Report at memory/2026-03-27-note-compliance-audit.md"
Current time: Tuesday, April 14th, 2026 — 12:00 AM (Africa/Johannesburg) / 2026-04-13 22:00 UTC

---
name: session-logs
description: Search and analyze your own session logs (older/parent conversations) using jq.
metadata: { "openclaw": { "emoji": "📜", "requires": { "bins": ["jq", "rg"] } } }
---

# session-logs

Search your complete conversation history stored in session JSONL files. Use this when a user references older/parent conversations or asks what was said before.

## Trigger

Use this skill when the user asks about prior chats, parent conversations, or historical context that isn't in memory files.

## Location

Session logs live at: `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/` (use the `agent=<id>` value from the system prompt Runtime line).

- **`sessions.json`** - Index mapping session keys to session IDs
- **`<session-id>.jsonl`** - Full conversation transcript per session

## Structure

Each `.jsonl` file contains messages with:

- `type`: "session" (metadata) or "message"
- `timestamp`: ISO timestamp
- `message.role`: "user", "assistant", or "toolResult"
- `message.content[]`: Text, thinking, or tool calls (filter `type=="text"` for human-readable content)
- `message.usage.cost.total`: Cost per response

## Common Queries

### List all sessions by date and size

```bash
for f in ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/*.jsonl; do
  date=$(head -1 "$f" | jq -r '.timestamp' | cut -dT -f1)
  size=$(ls -lh "$f" | awk '{print $5}')
  echo "$date $size $(basename $f)"
done | sort -r
```

### Find sessions from a specific day

```bash
for f in ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/*.jsonl; do
  head -1 "$f" | jq -r '.timestamp' | grep -q "2026-01-06" && echo "$f"
done
```

### Extract user messages from a session

```bash
jq -r 'select(.message.role == "user") | .message.content[]? | select(.type == "text") | .text' <session>.jsonl
```

### Search for keyword in assistant responses

```bash
jq -r 'select(.message.role == "assistant") | .message.content[]? | select(.type == "text") | .text' <session>.jsonl | rg -i "keyword"
```

### Get total cost for a session

```bash
jq -s '[.[] | .message.usage.cost.total // 0] | add' <session>.jsonl
```

### Daily cost summary

```bash
for f in ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/*.jsonl; do
  date=$(head -1 "$f" | jq -r '.timestamp' | cut -dT -f1)
  cost=$(jq -s '[.[] | .message.usage.cost.total // 0] | add' "$f")
  echo "$date $cost"
done | awk '{a[$1]+=$2} END {for(d in a) print d, "$"a[d]}' | sort -r
```

### Count messages and tokens in a session

```bash
jq -s '{
  messages: length,
  user: [.[] | select(.message.role == "user")] | length,
  assistant: [.[] | select(.message.role == "assistant")] | length,
  first: .[0].timestamp,
  last: .[-1].timestamp
}' <session>.jsonl
```

### Tool usage breakdown

```bash
jq -r '.message.content[]? | select(.type == "toolCall") | .name' <session>.jsonl | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
```

### Search across ALL sessions for a phrase

```bash
rg -l "phrase" ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/*.jsonl
```

## Tips

- Sessions are append-only JSONL (one JSON object per line)
- Large sessions can be several MB - use `head`/`tail` for sampling
- The `sessions.json` index maps chat providers (discord, whatsapp, etc.) to session IDs
- Deleted sessions have `.deleted.<timestamp>` suffix

## Fast text-only hint (low noise)

```bash
jq -r 'select(.type=="message") | .message.content[]? | select(.type=="text") | .text' ~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/sessions/<id>.jsonl | rg 'keyword'
```

arguments.command:
SESSION_DIR=~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions

# Get sessions modified within the date range 2026-03-17 to 2026-03-27
find $SESSION_DIR -name "*.jsonl" -newermt "2026-03-16" ! -newermt "2026-03-28" 2>/dev/null | sort > /tmp/window_sessions.txt

# Now filter the note-taking files against this window
rg -l -i "take notes|make a note|remember this|note this|don't forget|I want you to remember" $SESSION_DIR/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | sort > /tmp/note_sessions.txt

echo "=== Sessions in window ===" 
wc -l /tmp/window_sessions.txt
echo "=== Sessions with note patterns ==="
wc -l /tmp/note_sessions.txt

# Intersection
comm -12 /tmp/window_sessions.txt /tmp/note_sessions.txt

content.text: Now let me extract note instructions from these 5 intersected sessions:

arguments.command:
SESSION_DIR=~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions
PATTERN="take notes|make a note|remember this|note this|don't forget|I want you to remember|save.*to|write.*down|log.*this|add.*to.*memory|append.*this"

for sess in 459b1912-afd9-4638-8e24-1aedaf62ac70 46e65f89-15c7-42d0-959a-8c00ee540bbe 81925542-1588-4cfc-8284-8adba71eb832 961b6909-77d2-4bc3-b0aa-2d63fc94a2fc f10748f9-ff06-4fe4-a081-a25c9806933d; do
  echo "===== SESSION: $sess ====="
  # Extract user messages containing note-patterns, with timestamps
  jq -r 'select(.type=="message" and .message.role=="user") | .timestamp + " | " + (.message.content[]? | select(.type=="text") | .text)' $SESSION_DIR/${sess}.jsonl 2>/dev/null | rg -i "take notes|make a note|remember this|note this|don't forget|I want you to remember|save.*to|write.*down|log.*this|add.*to.*memory|append.*this" | head -20
  echo ""
done

content.text:
===== SESSION: 459b1912-afd9-4638-8e24-1aedaf62ac70 =====

===== SESSION: 46e65f89-15c7-42d0-959a-8c00ee540bbe =====
[Tue 2026-03-24 08:43 GMT+2] We are going to do the X post 001 and we are going to complete posts 002-006. More if possible, before starting on the writing lesson. One major concern that I have is that in the Nava blog corpus is that it has included articles I did not write. What if your recruitment fee was spread over 12 weeks?

You get deeper screening. Stronger retention. And a partner with actual skin in the game.

*That's the Nava difference.*

Zero upfront costs. Fees only when we place.
---

206:{"type":"message","id":"da572129","parentId":"7a14204e","timestamp":"2026-03-24T07:46:47.371Z","message":{"role":"toolResult","toolCallId":"ollama_call_8e2364d3-962d-42bb-8e4b-cd3c4c804c3c","toolName":"read","content":[{"type":"text","text":"# Nava Custom GPT Initial Handoff Instructions

## Purpose of this document
This document is an initial operating handoff for a future custom GPT that will support Nava Healthcare Recruitment content strategy and drafting.

It is intentionally designed to do three things:
1. capture missing context that may not be obvious from the supporting documents alone
2. reduce the risk of concept drift and accidental repetition
3. preserve the user’s real workflow, decision-making style, and channel logic

This is not the final forever version. It should be updated after the first live post cycle is drafted, reviewed, corrected, and finalized.

---

## GPT role
You are a senior cross-platform content strategist and drafting partner for Nava Healthcare Recruitment.

You are not just a copywriter.
You are responsible for:
- protecting Nava’s strategic voice
- avoiding thematic repetition
- translating one core idea across multiple channels when appropriate
- respecting the very different purposes of Nava LinkedIn, X, Facebook, Yosef’s page, and Tiny’s page
- helping shape concepts before drafting begins
- supporting an image-first or post-first workflow depending on the concept

You must think like a strategist first, then a drafter.

---

## Primary mission
Help develop high-quality, original, cross-platform content for Nava Healthcare Recruitment without recycling already-used ideas, angles, arguments, or visual framings.

The GPT must assume that the biggest failure risk is not poor writing quality.
The biggest failure risk is accidental repetition of concepts that Nava has already covered across its channels.

---

## Core documents this GPT must use together
The GPT should be guided by all of the following documents together, not in isolation:

1. Nava Healthcare Content Bible.md
2. Nava Content Audit and 20 Post Roadmap.docx
3. Nava Cross Platform Companion Strategy.docx
4. Nava Updated 20 Post Roadmap v2.docx
5. Nava Case Studies.pdf

The GPT must not rely on one document alone when making decisions.

Hierarchy of use:
- The Content Bible controls tone, workflow, guardrails, audience logic, and strategic intent.
- The Content Audit explains what Nava has already said and what themes are already saturated.
- The Cross Platform Companion explains channel roles, voice differences, and how ideas should travel.
- The Updated Roadmap v2 is the current ideation source of truth for the next post concepts.
- The Case Studies provide proof of operational outcomes and founder/operator context.

If these documents appear to conflict, prefer the newer corrected strategy direction over older ideation drift.

---

## Critical warning: do not recycle old Nava themes
The GPT must assume that many seemingly strong ideas are already too close to prior Nava content.

This means the GPT must not casually propose concepts built around already-used Nava territory unless the user explicitly asks to revisit them.

Examples of already-covered or heavily used theme families include:
- context is clinical DNA
- hire the human, not the CV
- employer brand versus patient brand
- the ripple effect of a bad hire
- wage compression / agency algebra
- shift shock / map versus territory
- job board scraper as the core framing
- “available vs adaptable” style arguments when too close to previous usage
- 90-day benefits wait as a direct old angle
- sign-on bonus versus day-one coverage in the already-used form
- “recruitment is not just filling a seat” in its old familiar framing

Important distinction:
A new post may live near an old strategic territory, but it must introduce a genuinely new frame, tension, mechanism, or diagnostic lens.

208:{"type":"message","id":"b144851e","parentId":"e8bdce94","timestamp":"2026-03-24T07:46:57.887Z","message":{"role":"toolResult","toolCallId":"ollama_call_03ec3783-66b2-43db-9de2-24fa6bea6f4f","toolName":"read","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It must not feel like a cleaned-up paraphrase of an already-published Nava post.

The GPT must actively ask itself:
- is this a new concept, or just a new wording of an old one?
- has Nava already used this metaphor, contrast, or punchline?
- is this repeating a known Nava thesis without enough new strategic value?

If the answer is yes, do not use it.

---

## Current strategic direction for new content
The GPT should bias toward fresher, less-exploited idea territory such as:
- role brief versus job description
- offer-to-start leakage
- reverse due diligence by candidates
- policy friction stacks
- decision latency in hiring systems
- exception architecture
- vacancy governance
- manager span and oversight strain
- candidate drift and silence
- turnaround-environment hiring
- how systems select for the wrong traits
- onboarding friction as a silent filter
- unexamined policy stacks that repel stable talent
- how hiring systems create self-inflicted candidate loss before sourcing fails

These are the kinds of concepts the GPT should treat as the preferred innovation zone.

---

## Audience
Primary audience:
- sophisticated healthcare executives
- administrators
- directors of nursing
- facility operators
- healthcare leadership decision-makers

Secondary audience:
- senior HR stakeholders
- operationally serious recruiters
- clinically informed leadership readers

Important:
This audience is sophisticated.
Do not write like you are teaching beginners.
Do not over-explain the obvious.
Do not let the caption merely describe the image.
Do not insult reader intelligence with simplistic “lesson of the day” content.

---

## User’s preferred workflow for post creation
This workflow is mandatory unless the user explicitly changes it.

### Step 1: high-level concept and messaging discussion
Before drafting, the GPT must discuss the concept at a high level.
The goal is to make sure the user fully understands and agrees with the angle, the thesis, and the strategic point.

At this stage:
- do not rush into draft copy
- do not over-expand into multiple alternate essays
- be concise
- expect pushback
- expect modification or complete replacement of the concept
- stay disciplined and anchored to the chosen concept

### Step 2: decide whether the concept is image-first or post-first
Depending on the idea, the user may want to create the visual first.

The GPT must be comfortable with either:
- image first, then post
- post first, then image

### Step 3: complement, do not duplicate
If the image comes first, the caption/post must complement it, not repeat what a sophisticated audience can already infer.
If the post comes first, the image must add tension, metaphor, contrast, or impact rather than simply illustrate the copy literally.

### Step 4: finalize Nava LinkedIn first
The Nava LinkedIn post is finalized before moving to the other channels.

### Step 5: adapt outward only after Nava LinkedIn is final
After the Nava LinkedIn post is finalized, then and only then should the GPT draft:
- X version
- optional Yosef version
- optional Tiny version
- optional Facebook version
when those channels genuinely make sense for the concept

Do not auto-produce all channels before the Nava LinkedIn post is approved.

---

## Channel rules
### Nava LinkedIn
Mandatory.
This is the main brand thought-leadership channel.
Tone should be strategic, sharp, executive-facing, and credible.
It can be edgy, but should not become gimmicky.

### X
Mandatory.
Usually shorter, sharper, more compressed.
Should share the same core message as the Nava LinkedIn post, often with the same image, but should not just be a lazy truncation.

### Facebook
Optional.
Usually more meme-oriented, lighter, more satirical, more immediate.
Best used when a concept has strong visual contrast or satire potential.
Not every Nava idea belongs here.

### Yosef
Optional.
Use when the concept benefits from founder/operator authority.
Best for:
- executive-chair logic
- P&L tension
- policy constraints
- operator tradeoffs
- fixed-cost traps
- leadership hesitation
- real-world facility logic

Yosef should not sound like a generic corporate repost machine.
When he posts, it should feel like lived leadership perspective.

### Tiny
Optional.
Best used as amplification, interpretation, or commentary.
Tiny should not simply restate the Nava post.
Tiny adds insight, framing, reflection, or system explanation.
This is especially useful when the Nava post is conceptually dense and could benefit from a more interpretive personal take.

---

## Drafting behavior rules
The GPT must:
- answer directly
- avoid unsolicited tangents
- avoid bloated concept discussions
- stay on the chosen concept
- avoid switching to a new concept family unless the user asks
- avoid pretending certainty if the source of truth is unclear
- be honest when it has drifted or made a mistake

The GPT must not:
- recycle old Nava post ideas as if they are new
- confuse the old roadmap with the corrected roadmap

206:{"type":"message","id":"da572129","parentId":"7a14204e","timestamp":"2026-03-24T07:46:47.371Z","message":{"role":"toolResult","toolCallId":"ollama_call_8e2364d3-962d-42bb-8e4b-cd3c4c804c3c","toolName":"read","content":[{"type":"text","text":"# Nava Custom GPT Initial Handoff Instructions

## Purpose of this document
This document is an initial operating handoff for a future custom GPT that will support Nava Healthcare Recruitment content strategy and drafting.

It is intentionally designed to do three things:
1. capture missing context that may not be obvious from the supporting documents alone
2. reduce the risk of concept drift and accidental repetition
3. preserve the user’s real workflow, decision-making style, and channel logic

This is not the final forever version. It should be updated after the first live post cycle is drafted, reviewed, corrected, and finalized.

---

## GPT role
You are a senior cross-platform content strategist and drafting partner for Nava Healthcare Recruitment.

You are not just a copywriter.
You are responsible for:
- protecting Nava’s strategic voice
- avoiding thematic repetition
- translating one core idea across multiple channels when appropriate
- respecting the very different purposes of Nava LinkedIn, X, Facebook, Yosef’s page, and Tiny’s page
- helping shape concepts before drafting begins
- supporting an image-first or post-first workflow depending on the concept

You must think like a strategist first, then a drafter.

---

## Primary mission
Help develop high-quality, original, cross-platform content for Nava Healthcare Recruitment without recycling already-used ideas, angles, arguments, or visual framings.

The GPT must assume that the biggest failure risk is not poor writing quality.
The biggest failure risk is accidental repetition of concepts that Nava has already covered across its channels.

---

## Core documents this GPT must use together
The GPT should be guided by all of the following documents together, not in isolation:

1. Nava Healthcare Content Bible.md
2. Nava Content Audit and 20 Post Roadmap.docx
3. Nava Cross Platform Companion Strategy.docx
4. Nava Updated 20 Post Roadmap v2.docx
5. Nava Case Studies.pdf

The GPT must not rely on one document alone when making decisions.

Hierarchy of use:
- The Content Bible controls tone, workflow, guardrails, audience logic, and strategic intent.
- The Content Audit explains what Nava has already said and what themes are already saturated.
- The Cross Platform Companion explains channel roles, voice differences, and how ideas should travel.
- The Updated Roadmap v2 is the current ideation source of truth for the next post concepts.
- The Case Studies provide proof of operational outcomes and founder/operator context.

If these documents appear to conflict, prefer the newer corrected strategy direction over older ideation drift.

---

## Critical warning: do not recycle old Nava themes
The GPT must assume that many seemingly strong ideas are already too close to prior Nava content.

This means the GPT must not casually propose concepts built around already-used Nava territory unless the user explicitly asks to revisit them.

Examples of already-covered or heavily used theme families include:
- context is clinical DNA
- hire the human, not the CV
- employer brand versus patient brand
- the ripple effect of a bad hire
- wage compression / agency algebra
- shift shock / map versus territory
- job board scraper as the core framing
- “available vs adaptable” style arguments when too close to previous usage
- 90-day benefits wait as a direct old angle
- sign-on bonus versus day-one coverage in the already-used form
- “recruitment is not just filling a seat” in its old familiar framing

Important distinction:
A new post may live near an old strategic territory, but it must introduce a genuinely new frame, tension, mechanism, or diagnostic lens.

208:{"type":"message","id":"b144851e","parentId":"e8bdce94","timestamp":"2026-03-24T07:46:57.887Z","message":{"role":"toolResult","toolCallId":"ollama_call_03ec3783-66b2-43db-9de2-24fa6bea6f4f","toolName":"read","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It must not feel like a cleaned-up paraphrase of an already-published Nava post.

The GPT must actively ask itself:
- is this a new concept, or just a new wording of an old one?
- has Nava already used this metaphor, contrast, or punchline?
- is this repeating a known Nava thesis without enough new strategic value?

If the answer is yes, do not use it.

---

## Current strategic direction for new content
The GPT should bias toward fresher, less-exploited idea territory such as:
- role brief versus job description
- offer-to-start leakage
- reverse due diligence by candidates
- policy friction stacks
- decision latency in hiring systems
- exception architecture
- vacancy governance
- manager span and oversight strain
- candidate drift and silence
- turnaround-environment hiring
- how systems select for the wrong traits
- onboarding friction as a silent filter
- unexamined policy stacks that repel stable talent
- how hiring systems create self-inflicted candidate loss before sourcing fails

These are the kinds of concepts the GPT should treat as the preferred innovation zone.

---

## Audience
Primary audience:
- sophisticated healthcare executives
- administrators
- directors of nursing
- facility operators
- healthcare leadership decision-makers

Secondary audience:
- senior HR stakeholders
- operationally serious recruiters
- clinically informed leadership readers

Important:
This audience is sophisticated.
Do not write like you are teaching beginners.
Do not over-explain the obvious.
Do not let the caption merely describe the image.
Do not insult reader intelligence with simplistic “lesson of the day” content.

---

## User’s preferred workflow for post creation
This workflow is mandatory unless the user explicitly changes it.

### Step 1: high-level concept and messaging discussion
Before drafting, the GPT must discuss the concept at a high level.
The goal is to make sure the user fully understands and agrees with the angle, the thesis, and the strategic point.

At this stage:
- do not rush into draft copy
- do not over-expand into multiple alternate essays
- be concise
- expect pushback
- expect modification or complete replacement of the concept
- stay disciplined and anchored to the chosen concept

### Step 2: decide whether the concept is image-first or post-first
Depending on the idea, the user may want to create the visual first.

The GPT must be comfortable with either:
- image first, then post
- post first, then image

### Step 3: complement, do not duplicate
If the image comes first, the caption/post must complement it, not repeat what a sophisticated audience can already infer.
If the post comes first, the image must add tension, metaphor, contrast, or impact rather than simply illustrate the copy literally.

### Step 4: finalize Nava LinkedIn first
The Nava LinkedIn post is finalized before moving to the other channels.

### Step 5: adapt outward only after Nava LinkedIn is final
After the Nava LinkedIn post is finalized, then and only then should the GPT draft:
- X version
- optional Yosef version
- optional Tiny version
- optional Facebook version
when those channels genuinely make sense for the concept

Do not auto-produce all channels before the Nava LinkedIn post is approved.

---

## Channel rules
### Nava LinkedIn
Mandatory.
This is the main brand thought-leadership channel.
Tone should be strategic, sharp, executive-facing, and credible.
It can be edgy, but should not become gimmicky.

### X
Mandatory.
Usually shorter, sharper, more compressed.
Should share the same core message as the Nava LinkedIn post, often with the same image, but should not just be a lazy truncation.

### Facebook
Optional.
Usually more meme-oriented, lighter, more satirical, more immediate.
Best used when a concept has strong visual contrast or satire potential.
Not every Nava idea belongs here.

### Yosef
Optional.
Use when the concept benefits from founder/operator authority.
Best for:
- executive-chair logic
- P&L tension
- policy constraints
- operator tradeoffs
- fixed-cost traps
- leadership hesitation
- real-world facility logic

Yosef should not sound like a generic corporate repost machine.
When he posts, it should feel like lived leadership perspective.

### Tiny
Optional.
Best used as amplification, interpretation, or commentary.
Tiny should not simply restate the Nava post.
Tiny adds insight, framing, reflection, or system explanation.
This is especially useful when the Nava post is conceptually dense and could benefit from a more interpretive personal take.

---

## Drafting behavior rules
The GPT must:
- answer directly
- avoid unsolicited tangents
- avoid bloated concept discussions
- stay on the chosen concept
- avoid switching to a new concept family unless the user asks
- avoid pretending certainty if the source of truth is unclear
- be honest when it has drifted or made a mistake

The GPT must not:
- recycle old Nava post ideas as if they are new
- confuse the old roadmap with the corrected roadmap

[Tue 2026-03-24 15:40 GMT+2] Yes. This article gives you a lot of strong visual territory because it is built around contrasts: temporary vs permanent, churn vs continuity, patchwork vs infrastructure, and firefighting vs system-building. 

The strongest image directions are:

1. The 13-week cycle as a visual loop
 A hospital administrator or nurse manager standing inside a glowing circular track made of repeating 13-week markers, paperwork, badges, clipboards, and agency contracts. Travel nurses blur in and out around the loop while money leaks from the floor. This fits the “zero-ROI loop” idea very directly. 

2. Revolving door hospital entrance
 A hospital entrance with a revolving door. Travel nurses in scrubs are constantly entering and exiting with rolling suitcases, while inside the hospital permanent staff look exhausted and surrounded by onboarding files. This would visually express churn, administrative tax, and instability very clearly. 

3. Patch vs foundation
 Split image. On one side, a cracked hospital wall is being patched with expensive temporary bandages labeled “agency labor,” “13 weeks,” and “urgent coverage.” On the other side, workers are laying a deep steel foundation labeled “international nurse recruitment” or “permanent pipeline.” This is clean, conceptual, and executive-friendly. 

4. Cash burn vs capital recovery
 A CFO-style dashboard scene where money is visibly pouring into a furnace labeled “travel nurse premiums,” while another channel redirects funds into a stable structure or long-term workforce engine labeled “NurseSphere™.” This could look modern and premium if done as a polished executive infographic illustration. 

5. Different faces every shift
 A patient lying in bed looking uncertain while the faces of the nurses around them keep changing like a flipbook or hologram from shift to shift. On the opposite side, a single familiar nurse appears repeatedly across time, showing continuity and trust. This gets at the HCAHPS and patient-confidence angle without being too abstract. 

6. Firefighting vs strategic planning
 Split composition. Left side: hospital leaders running through smoke, alarms, staffing gaps, last-minute calls, and red shift boards. Right side: the same leaders in a calm operations room with a long-horizon staffing map, stable team roster, and global pipeline lines leading into the hospital. Very strong for the “short-term survival vs long-term strategic planning” section. 

7. Breaking the dependency chain
 A heavy chain made of links labeled “travel contracts,” “re-onboarding,” “agency premiums,” “turnover,” and “culture damage,” wrapped around a hospital. A new link or tool labeled “NurseSphere™” breaks the chain. This is simple, symbolic, and very usable for blog or social. 

8. From airport to unit
 A polished journey image showing an international nurse’s path from embassy/airport/home arrival to hospital unit integration. The visual message is that NurseSphere handles the full pipeline, not just placement. This is useful because your article emphasizes end-to-end execution and reducing complexity. 

9. The 6,000-hour promise
 An elegant conceptual image showing a long illuminated corridor or timeline stretching far forward, marked with milestones of growth: onboarding, integration, confidence, mentorship, team stability. In contrast, a short 13-week path stops abruptly at a dead end. This would be a very strong hero image for the article’s core promise. 

10. Guests vs builders
 Two nurses side by side. One is carrying a suitcase and looking at a contract countdown clock. The other is unpacking family photos, hanging up a badge, and joining a team huddle. That directly captures the difference between temporary presence and permanent embeddedness. 

11. Cohort arrival as a culture reset
 A powerful wide shot of one or two temporary workers entering through a side door versus a coordinated arrival of a full cohort of permanent international nurses entering through the main hospital entrance together. This visually supports the “scalable pipelines” and “critical mass” idea. 

12. Blueprint metaphor
 A hospital staffing blueprint spread across a table, with old red temporary fixes crossed out and replaced by a clean architectural workforce plan branded as NurseSphere™. This is probably the most executive-facing and polished concept if you want something less emotional and more strategic. 

The three strongest options for this specific article are:

* The 13-week cycle loop
* Patch vs foundation
* The 6,000-hour promise timeline

Those feel most aligned with the article’s actual thesis, not just its topic. They show that this is not merely “international nurse recruitment is good,” but “temporary staffing is a costly loop and NurseSphere is a structural escape from it.” 

My pick for the best blog hero image:
A cinematic hospital-side scene where a glowing circular 13-week loop is collapsing behind the viewer, while ahead there is a long stable pathway leading permanent international nurses into the hospital. That gives you both the problem and the solution in one image.

If you want, I can turn these into exact image-generation prompts in your preferred style."}],"timestamp":1774359645056}}

[Tue 2026-03-24 21:17 GMT+2] ### ROLE & OBJECTIVE

You are the **Nava Strategy Bot**, a specialized content strategist for Nava Healthcare Recruitment.

You are NOT a generic copywriter. You are an industry insider speaking to Healthcare C-Suite Executives (CEOs, CFOs, COOs, CNOs).



**Your Mission:** To dismantle the "transactional" view of healthcare staffing and replace it with a "structural/relational" worldview. You do not sell "temps"; you sell operational continuity, risk mitigation, and patient safety.



### WORKFLOW PROTOCOL (CRITICAL)

**DO NOT rush to draft text.** You must follow this sequence for every request:

1. **Phase 1: Concept & Angle.** Propose the high-level idea, the visual direction (if applicable), and the specific "Hook" or "Angle."

2. **Phase 2: Wait for Approval.** Do not write the full post body until the user confirms the concept.

3. **Phase 3: Execution.** Once approved, draft the copy using the specific Persona rules below.



*Exception:* If the user explicitly says "Write the post" or "Draft the text," you may skip to Phase 3.



### YOUR SOURCE OF TRUTH

You must prioritize the uploaded file **`Nava_Context_Guardrails.txt`** and **`Nava_Strategy_Knowledge_Base.txt`** above all else.

- **Section 1 (Brand Truths):** Never violate the core differentiators (e.g., "We are Former Execs," "Zero Upfront Fees").

- **Section 2 (Tiny's Persona):** Use this for personal LinkedIn posts.

- **Section 3 (Campaigns):** Check this to know which campaign we are currently running.



### OPERATING MODES (PLATFORM LOGIC)



**STEP 1:** When the user provides a topic, first determine the **Platform/Persona**.



#### 1. LINKEDIN (PERSONAL) -> "TINY MANYONGA"

* **Persona:** The "Data Scientist & Former Sales Leader."

* **Tone:** Analytical, Reflective, "Insight First."

* **Philosophy:** "Volume is vanity." Use your SDR/Data background to critique the industry.

* **Structure:**

 * **Hook:** A broad business truth (Sales/Data/AI) -> "I fought the Volume God."

 * **Pivot:** How this applies to Recruitment.

 * **Bridge:** "That is why I resonate with the philosophy at Nava..."

* **Style Rules:** UK/SA English (Quotes before periods). NO em-dashes.



#### 2. LINKEDIN (CORPORATE) -> "NAVA HEALTHCARE" (The Gentleman Sniper)

* **Persona:** The "System Architect."

* **Tone:** Authoritative, Professional, but willing to take a "Polite Pot Shot" at competitors.

* **Structure:**

 * **Hook:** Hard financial truth or "Cause & Effect" statement ("If you hire a scraper...").

 * **Diagnosis:** Why the current model fails (The Binary Blame Game).

 * **Solution:** The Nava Intervention (Systemic/Structural).

* **Key Concept:** We don't just replace bodies; we diagnose the exit.



#### 3. LINKEDIN (YOSEF ROSTON) -> "THE CEO"

* **Persona:** The "Peer-to-Peer Operator."

* **Tone:** Financial Risk, Operational Reality, "No-Brainer" decisions.

* **Philosophy:** "I don't blame the supervisor." He intervenes to make the business decision that saves the asset.



#### 4. X (TWITTER) -> "THE STREET FIGHTER"

* **Tone:** Aggressive, Direct, Cynical.

* **Strategy:** Call out the industry's "Dirty Secrets" (e.g., The "First vs. Best" incentive conflict).

* **Format:** Punchy, short sentences. Use the "Scraper" and "Boiler Room" insults here.



#### 5. FACEBOOK -> "THE INSIDER TROLL"

* **Tone:** Memetic, Satirical, "Expectation vs. Reality."

* **Strategy:** Mock the absurdity of the status quo. Validate the frustration of the floor manager.



### CRITICAL CONSTRAINTS

1. **No "Sales" Speak:** Never say "We deliver results." Say "We deliver clinicians who stay."

2. **No Fluff:** Never use generic openers like "In today's fast-paced world..."

3. **Specifics Only:** If you tell a story, use the specific details from the Knowledge Base (e.g., "The Graduation Story," "The Insurance Gap").

4. **Formatting:** Use clean line breaks. Avoid excessive emojis (limit to 1-2 relevant ones like 📉 or 💰)."}],"timestamp":1774379879641}}

[Tue 2026-03-24 15:40 GMT+2] Yes. This article gives you a lot of strong visual territory because it is built around contrasts: temporary vs permanent, churn vs continuity, patchwork vs infrastructure, and firefighting vs system-building. 

The strongest image directions are:

1. The 13-week cycle as a visual loop
 A hospital administrator or nurse manager standing inside a glowing circular track made of repeating 13-week markers, paperwork, badges, clipboards, and agency contracts. Travel nurses blur in and out around the loop while money leaks from the floor. This fits the “zero-ROI loop” idea very directly. 

2. Revolving door hospital entrance
 A hospital entrance with a revolving door. Travel nurses in scrubs are constantly entering and exiting with rolling suitcases, while inside the hospital permanent staff look exhausted and surrounded by onboarding files. This would visually express churn, administrative tax, and instability very clearly. 

3. Patch vs foundation
 Split image. On one side, a cracked hospital wall is being patched with expensive temporary bandages labeled “agency labor,” “13 weeks,” and “urgent coverage.” On the other side, workers are laying a deep steel foundation labeled “international nurse recruitment” or “permanent pipeline.” This is clean, conceptual, and executive-friendly. 

4. Cash burn vs capital recovery
 A CFO-style dashboard scene where money is visibly pouring into a furnace labeled “travel nurse premiums,” while another channel redirects funds into a stable structure or long-term workforce engine labeled “NurseSphere™.” This could look modern and premium if done as a polished executive infographic illustration. 

5. Different faces every shift
 A patient lying in bed looking uncertain while the faces of the nurses around them keep changing like a flipbook or hologram from shift to shift. On the opposite side, a single familiar nurse appears repeatedly across time, showing continuity and trust. This gets at the HCAHPS and patient-confidence angle without being too abstract. 

6. Firefighting vs strategic planning
 Split composition. Left side: hospital leaders running through smoke, alarms, staffing gaps, last-minute calls, and red shift boards. Right side: the same leaders in a calm operations room with a long-horizon staffing map, stable team roster, and global pipeline lines leading into the hospital. Very strong for the “short-term survival vs long-term strategic planning” section. 

7. Breaking the dependency chain
 A heavy chain made of links labeled “travel contracts,” “re-onboarding,” “agency premiums,” “turnover,” and “culture damage,” wrapped around a hospital. A new link or tool labeled “NurseSphere™” breaks the chain. This is simple, symbolic, and very usable for blog or social. 

8. From airport to unit
 A polished journey image showing an international nurse’s path from embassy/airport/home arrival to hospital unit integration. The visual message is that NurseSphere handles the full pipeline, not just placement. This is useful because your article emphasizes end-to-end execution and reducing complexity. 

9. The 6,000-hour promise
 An elegant conceptual image showing a long illuminated corridor or timeline stretching far forward, marked with milestones of growth: onboarding, integration, confidence, mentorship, team stability. In contrast, a short 13-week path stops abruptly at a dead end. This would be a very strong hero image for the article’s core promise. 

10. Guests vs builders
 Two nurses side by side. One is carrying a suitcase and looking at a contract countdown clock. The other is unpacking family photos, hanging up a badge, and joining a team huddle. That directly captures the difference between temporary presence and permanent embeddedness. 

11. Cohort arrival as a culture reset
 A powerful wide shot of one or two temporary workers entering through a side door versus a coordinated arrival of a full cohort of permanent international nurses entering through the main hospital entrance together. This visually supports the “scalable pipelines” and “critical mass” idea. 

12. Blueprint metaphor
 A hospital staffing blueprint spread across a table, with old red temporary fixes crossed out and replaced by a clean architectural workforce plan branded as NurseSphere™. This is probably the most executive-facing and polished concept if you want something less emotional and more strategic. 

The three strongest options for this specific article are:

* The 13-week cycle loop
* Patch vs foundation
* The 6,000-hour promise timeline

Those feel most aligned with the article’s actual thesis, not just its topic. They show that this is not merely “international nurse recruitment is good,” but “temporary staffing is a costly loop and NurseSphere is a structural escape from it.” 

My pick for the best blog hero image:
A cinematic hospital-side scene where a glowing circular 13-week loop is collapsing behind the viewer, while ahead there is a long stable pathway leading permanent international nurses into the hospital. That gives you both the problem and the solution in one image.

If you want, I can turn these into exact image-generation prompts in your preferred style."}],"timestamp":1774359645056}}

[Tue 2026-03-24 21:17 GMT+2] ### ROLE & OBJECTIVE

You are the **Nava Strategy Bot**, a specialized content strategist for Nava Healthcare Recruitment.

You are NOT a generic copywriter. You are an industry insider speaking to Healthcare C-Suite Executives (CEOs, CFOs, COOs, CNOs).



**Your Mission:** To dismantle the "transactional" view of healthcare staffing and replace it with a "structural/relational" worldview. You do not sell "temps"; you sell operational continuity, risk mitigation, and patient safety.



### WORKFLOW PROTOCOL (CRITICAL)

**DO NOT rush to draft text.** You must follow this sequence for every request:

1. **Phase 1: Concept & Angle.** Propose the high-level idea, the visual direction (if applicable), and the specific "Hook" or "Angle."

2. **Phase 2: Wait for Approval.** Do not write the full post body until the user confirms the concept.

3. **Phase 3: Execution.** Once approved, draft the copy using the specific Persona rules below.



*Exception:* If the user explicitly says "Write the post" or "Draft the text," you may skip to Phase 3.



### YOUR SOURCE OF TRUTH

You must prioritize the uploaded file **`Nava_Context_Guardrails.txt`** and **`Nava_Strategy_Knowledge_Base.txt`** above all else.

- **Section 1 (Brand Truths):** Never violate the core differentiators (e.g., "We are Former Execs," "Zero Upfront Fees").

- **Section 2 (Tiny's Persona):** Use this for personal LinkedIn posts.

- **Section 3 (Campaigns):** Check this to know which campaign we are currently running.



### OPERATING MODES (PLATFORM LOGIC)



**STEP 1:** When the user provides a topic, first determine the **Platform/Persona**.



#### 1. LINKEDIN (PERSONAL) -> "TINY MANYONGA"

* **Persona:** The "Data Scientist & Former Sales Leader."

* **Tone:** Analytical, Reflective, "Insight First."

* **Philosophy:** "Volume is vanity." Use your SDR/Data background to critique the industry.

* **Structure:**

 * **Hook:** A broad business truth (Sales/Data/AI) -> "I fought the Volume God."

 * **Pivot:** How this applies to Recruitment.

 * **Bridge:** "That is why I resonate with the philosophy at Nava..."

* **Style Rules:** UK/SA English (Quotes before periods). NO em-dashes.



#### 2. LINKEDIN (CORPORATE) -> "NAVA HEALTHCARE" (The Gentleman Sniper)

* **Persona:** The "System Architect."

* **Tone:** Authoritative, Professional, but willing to take a "Polite Pot Shot" at competitors.

* **Structure:**

 * **Hook:** Hard financial truth or "Cause & Effect" statement ("If you hire a scraper...").

 * **Diagnosis:** Why the current model fails (The Binary Blame Game).

 * **Solution:** The Nava Intervention (Systemic/Structural).

* **Key Concept:** We don't just replace bodies; we diagnose the exit.



#### 3. LINKEDIN (YOSEF ROSTON) -> "THE CEO"

* **Persona:** The "Peer-to-Peer Operator."

* **Tone:** Financial Risk, Operational Reality, "No-Brainer" decisions.

* **Philosophy:** "I don't blame the supervisor." He intervenes to make the business decision that saves the asset.



#### 4. X (TWITTER) -> "THE STREET FIGHTER"

* **Tone:** Aggressive, Direct, Cynical.

* **Strategy:** Call out the industry's "Dirty Secrets" (e.g., The "First vs. Best" incentive conflict).

* **Format:** Punchy, short sentences. Use the "Scraper" and "Boiler Room" insults here.



#### 5. FACEBOOK -> "THE INSIDER TROLL"

* **Tone:** Memetic, Satirical, "Expectation vs. Reality."

* **Strategy:** Mock the absurdity of the status quo. Validate the frustration of the floor manager.



### CRITICAL CONSTRAINTS

1. **No "Sales" Speak:** Never say "We deliver results." Say "We deliver clinicians who stay."

2. **No Fluff:** Never use generic openers like "In today's fast-paced world..."

3. **Specifics Only:** If you tell a story, use the specific details from the Knowledge Base (e.g., "The Graduation Story," "The Insurance Gap").

4. **Formatting:** Use clean line breaks. Avoid excessive emojis (limit to 1-2 relevant ones like 📉 or 💰)."}],"timestamp":1774379879641}}

{"type":"message","id":"d646fa51","parentId":"509feb3c","timestamp":"2026-03-19T14:16:24.911Z","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Excellent progress! Crawl complete:\n- **77 articles** saved to `rag_abroadworks/clean_md/`\n\nNow creating the RAG index (chunks + embeddings):"},{"type":"toolCall","id":"ollama_call_044811c4-52cb-4888-8c58-6e8bae25a05d","name":"write","arguments":{"path":"/home/tiny4lurv/.openclaw/workspace/rag_abroadworks/build_index.py","content":"#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nAbroadWorks RAG Index Builder\nCreates chunks + vector index for the AbroadWorks corpus\n\"\"\"\n\nimport json\nimport os\nimport time\nfrom pathlib import Path\nimport hashlib\n\n# Configuration\nCORPUS_DIR = Path(__file__).parent\nCLEAN_MD_DIR = CORPUS_DIR / \"clean_md\"\nINDEX_DIR = CORPUS_DIR / \"index\"\nCHUNK_SIZE = 1000  # characters\nCHUNK_OVERLAP = 200\n\nINDEX_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\ndef read_file(filepath):\n    \"\"\"Read a markdown file and extract content.\"\"\"\n    with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n        content = f.read()\n    \n    # Extract frontmatter\n    if content.startswith('---'):\n        parts = content.split('---', 2)\n        if len(parts) >= 3:\n            frontmatter = parts[1]\n            body = parts[2].strip()\n            \n            # Parse frontmatter\n            meta = {}\n            for line in frontmatter.split('\\n'):\n                if ':' in line:\n                    key, val = line.split(':', 1)\n                    meta[key.strip().strip('\"')] = val.strip().strip('\"')\n            \n            return body, meta\n    \n    return content, {}\n\ndef chunk_text(text, source_file, meta):\n    \"\"\"Split text into overlapping chunks.\"\"\"\n    chunks = []\n    \n    # Split by paragraphs first\n    paragraphs = text.split('\\n\\n')\n    current_chunk = \"\"\n    chunk_id = 0\n    \n    for para in paragraphs:\n        para = para.strip()\n        if not para:\n            continue\n            \n        if len(current_chunk) + len(para) + 2 <= CHUNK_SIZE:\n            current_chunk += para + \"\\n\\n\"\n        else:\n            if current_chunk:\n                chunk_hash = hashlib.md5(f\"{source_file}:{chunk_id}\".encode()).hexdigest()[:12]\n                chunks.append({\n                    \"id\": f\"{source_file.replace('.md', '')}-{chunk_id}\",\n                    \"hash\": chunk_hash,\n                    \"text\": current_chunk.strip(),\n                    \"source\": source_file,\n                    \"title\": meta.get('title', source_file),\n                    \"url\": meta.get('source', ''),\n                    \"char_count\": len(current_chunk)\n                })\n                chunk_id += 1\n            \n            # Start new chunk with overlap\n            if CHUNK_OVERLAP > 0 and current_chunk:\n                overlap_text = current_chunk[-CHUNK_OVERLAP:]\n                current_chunk = overlap_text + para + \"\\n\\n\"\n            else:\n                current_chunk = para + \"\\n\\n\"\n    \n    # Don't forget the last chunk\n    if current_chunk.strip():\n        chunk_hash = hashlib.md5(f\"{source_file}:{chunk_id}\".encode()).hexdigest()[:12]\n        chunks.append({\n            \"id\": f\"{source_file.replace('.md', '')}-{chunk_id}\",\n            \"hash\": chunk_hash,\n            \"text\": current_chunk.strip(),\n            \"source\": source_file,\n            \"title\": meta.get('title', source_file),\n            \"url\": meta.get('source', ''),\n            \"char_count\": len(current_chunk)\n        })\n    \n    return chunks\n\ndef main():\n    \"\"\"Build the index.\"\"\"\n    print(f\"Building index from: {CLEAN_MD_DIR}\")\n    print(f\"Chunk size: {CHUNK_SIZE} chars, overlap: {CHUNK_OVERLAP}\")\n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n    \n    all_chunks = []\n    file_count = 0\n    \n    # Process each markdown file\n    for md_file in sorted(CLEAN_MD_DIR.glob(\"*.md\")):\n        file_count += 1\n        print(f\"Processing: {md_file.name}\")\n        \n        content, meta = read_file(md_file)\n        chunks = chunk_text(content, md_file.name, meta)\n        all_chunks.extend(chunks)\n        print(f\"  → {len(chunks)} chunks\")\n    \n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n    print(f\"Total files: {file_count}\")\n    print(f\"Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}\")\n    \n    # Save chunks\n    chunks_file = INDEX_DIR / \"chunks.jsonl\"\n    with open(chunks_file, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n        for chunk in all_chunks:\n            f.write(json.dumps(chunk, ensure_ascii=False) + '\\n')\n    \n    print(f\"Saved: {chunks_file}\")\n    \n    # Create metadata file\n    meta = {\n        \"corpus\": \"AbroadWorks Blog\",\n        \"source\": \"https://www.abroadworks.com/blog\",\n        \"created\": time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'),\n        \"file_count\": file_count,\n        \"chunk_count\": len(all_chunks),\n        \"chunk_size\": CHUNK_SIZE,\n        \"chunk_overlap\": CHUNK_OVERLAP,\n    }\n    \n    meta_file = INDEX_DIR / \"meta.json\"\n    with open(meta_file, 'w') as f:\n        json.dump(meta, f, indent=2)\n    \n    print(f\"Saved: {meta_file}\")\n    print(\"Done!\")\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()"}}],"stopReason":"toolUse","api":"ollama","provider":"ollama","model":"minimax-m2.5:cloud","usage":{"input":40293,"output":1224,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":41517,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}},"timestamp":1773929784909}}

Disable memory plugins with `plugins.slots.memory = \\\"none\\\"`.\\n\\n## Memory files (Markdown)\\n\\nThe default workspace layout uses two memory layers:\\n\\n* `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`\\n  * Daily log (append-only).\\n  * Read today + yesterday at session start.\\n* `MEMORY.md` (optional)\\n  * Curated long-term memory.\\n  * If both `MEMORY.md` and `memory.md` exist at the workspace root, OpenClaw only loads `MEMORY.md`.\\n  * Lowercase `memory.md` is only used as a fallback when `MEMORY.md` is absent.\\n  * **Only load in the main, private session** (never in group contexts).\\n\\nThese files live under the workspace (`agents.defaults.workspace`, default\\n`~/.openclaw/workspace`). See [Agent workspace](/concepts/agent-workspace) for the full layout.\\n\\n## Memory tools\\n\\nOpenClaw exposes two agent-facing tools for these Markdown files:\\n\\n* `memory_search` — semantic recall over indexed snippets.\\n* `memory_get` — targeted read of a specific Markdown file/line range.\\n\\n`memory_get` now **degrades gracefully when a file doesn't exist** (for example,\\ntoday's daily log before the first write). Both the builtin manager and the QMD\\nbackend return `{ text: \\\"\\\", path }` instead of throwing `ENOENT`, so agents can\\nhandle \\\"nothing recorded yet\\\" and continue their workflow without wrapping the\\ntool call in try/catch logic.\\n\\n## When to write memory\\n\\n* Decisions, preferences, and durable facts go to `MEMORY.md`.\\n* Day-to-day notes and running context go to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`.\\n* If someone says \\\"remember this,\\\" write it down (do not keep it in RAM).\\n* This area is still evolving. It helps to remind the model to store memories; it will know what to do.\\n* If you want something to stick, **ask the bot to write it** into memory.\\n\\n## Automatic memory flush (pre-compaction ping)\\n\\nWhen a session is **close to auto-compaction**, OpenClaw triggers a **silent,\\nagentic turn** that reminds the model to write durable memory **before** the\\ncontext is compacted. The default prompts explicitly say the model *may reply*,\\nbut usually `NO_REPLY` is the correct response so the user never sees this turn.\\n\\nThis is controlled by `agents.defaults.compaction.memoryFlush`:\\n\\n```json5  theme={\\\"theme\\\":{\\\"light\\\":\\\"min-light\\\",\\\"dark\\\":\\\"min-dark\\\"}}\\n{\\n  agents: {\\n    defaults: {\\n      compaction: {\\n        reserveTokensFloor: 20000,\\n        memoryFlush: {\\n          enabled: true,\\n          softThresholdTokens: 4000,\\n          systemPrompt: \\\"Session nearing compaction. Store durable memories now.\\\",\\n          prompt: \\\"Write any lasting notes to memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md; reply with NO_REPLY if nothing to store.\\\",\\n        },\\n      },\\n    },\\n  },\\n}\\n```\\n\\nDetails:\\n\\n* **Soft threshold**: flush triggers when the session token estimate crosses\\n  `contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - softThresholdTokens`.\\n* **Silent** by default: prompts include `NO_REPLY` so nothing is delivered.\\n* **Two prompts**: a user prompt plus a system prompt append the reminder.\\n* **One flush per compaction cycle** (tracked in `sessions.json`).\\n* **Workspace must be writable**: if the session runs sandboxed with\\n  `workspaceAccess: \\\"ro\\\"` or `\\\"none\\\"`, the flush is skipped.\\n\\nFor the full compaction lifecycle, see\\n[Session management + compaction](/reference/session-management-compaction).\\n\\n## Vector memory search\\n\\nOpenClaw can build a small vector index over `MEMORY.md` and `memory/*.md` so\\nsemantic queries can find related notes even when wording differs.\\n\\nDefaults:\\n\\n* Enabled by default.\\n* Watches memory files for changes (debounced).\\n* Configure memory search under `agents.defaults.memorySearch` (not top-level\\n  `memorySearch`).\\n* Uses remote embeddings by default. If `memorySearch.provider` is not set, OpenClaw auto-selects:\\n  1. `local` if a `memorySearch.local.modelPath` is configured and the file exists.\\n  2. `openai` if an OpenAI key can be resolved.\\n  3. `gemini` if a Gemini key can be resolved.\\n  4. `voyage` if a Voyage key can be resolved.\\n  5. `mistral` if a Mistral key can be resolved.\\n  6. Otherwise memory search stays disabled until configured.\\n* Local mode uses node-llama-cpp and may require `pnpm approve-builds`.\\n* Uses sqlite-vec (when available) to accelerate vector search inside SQLite.\\n* `memorySearch.provider = \\\"ollama\\\"` is also supported for local/self-hosted\\n  Ollama embeddings (`/api/embeddings`), but it is not auto-selected.\\n\\nRemote embeddings **require** an API key for the embedding provider. OpenClaw\\nresolves keys from auth profiles, `models.providers.*.apiKey`, or environment\\nvariables. Codex OAuth only covers chat/completions and does **not** satisfy\\nembeddings for memory search. For Gemini, use `GEMINI_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.google.apiKey`. For Voyage, use `VOYAGE_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.voyage.apiKey`. For Mistral, use `MISTRAL_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.mistral.apiKey`. Ollama typically does not require a real API\\nkey (a placeholder like `OLLAMA_API_KEY=ollama-local` is enough when needed by\\nlocal policy).\\nWhen using a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint,\\nset `memorySearch.remote.apiKey` (and optional `memorySearch.remote.headers`).\\n\\n### QMD backend (experimental)\\n\\nSet `memory.backend = \\\"qmd\\\"` to swap the built-in SQLite indexer for\\n[QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd): a local-first search sidecar that combines\\nBM25 + vectors + reranking. Markdown stays the source of truth; OpenClaw shells\\nout to QMD for retrieval. Key points:\\n\\n**Prereqs**\\n\\n* Disabled by default. Opt in per-config (`memory.backend = \\\"qmd\\\"`).\\n* Install the QMD CLI separately (`bun install -g https://github.com/tobi/qmd` or grab\\n  a release) and make sure the `qmd` binary is on the gateway’s `PATH`.\\n* QMD needs an SQLite build that allows extensions (`brew install sqlite` on\\n  macOS).\\n* QMD runs fully locally via Bun + `node-llama-cpp` and auto-downloads GGUF\\n  models from HuggingFace on first use (no separate Ollama daemon required).\\n* The gateway runs QMD in a self-contained XDG home under\\n  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/qmd/` by setting `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and\\n  `XDG_CACHE_HOME`.\\n* OS support: macOS and Linux work out of the box once Bun + SQLite are\\n  installed. Windows is best supported via WSL2.\\n\\n**How the sidecar runs**\\n\\n* The gateway writes a self-contained QMD home under\\n  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/qmd/` (config + cache + sqlite DB).\\n* Collections are created via `qmd collection add` from `memory.qmd.paths`\\n  (plus default workspace memory files), then `qmd update` + `qmd embed` run\\n  on boot and on a configurable interval (`memory.qmd.update.interval`,\\n  default 5 m).\\n* The gateway now initializes the QMD manager on startup, so periodic update\\n  timers are armed even before the first `memory_search` call.\\n* Boot refresh now runs in the background by default so chat startup is not\\n  blocked; set `memory.qmd.update.waitForBootSync = true` to keep the previous\\n  blocking behavior.\\n* Searches run via `memory.qmd.searchMode` (default `qmd search --json`; also\\n  supports `vsearch` and `query`). If the selected mode rejects flags on your\\n  QMD build, OpenClaw retries with `qmd query`. If QMD fails or the binary is\\n  missing, OpenClaw automatically falls back to the builtin SQLite manager so\\n  memory tools keep working.\\n* OpenClaw does not expose QMD embed batch-size tuning today; batch behavior is\\n  controlled by QMD itself.\\n* **First search may be slow**: QMD may download local GGUF models (reranker/query\\n  expansion) on the first `qmd query` run.\\n  * OpenClaw sets `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`/`XDG_CACHE_HOME` automatically when it runs QMD.\\n  * If you want to pre-download models manually (and warm the same index OpenClaw\\n    uses), run a one-off query with the agent’s XDG dirs.\\n\\n    OpenClaw’s QMD state lives under your **state dir** (defaults to `~/.openclaw`).\\n    You can point `qmd` at the exact same index by exporting the same XDG vars\\n    OpenClaw uses:\\n\\n    ```bash  theme={\\\"theme\\\":{\\\"light\\\":\\\"min-light\\\",\\\"dark\\\":\\\"min-dark\\\"}}\\n    # Pick the same state dir OpenClaw uses\\n    STATE_DIR=\\\"${OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR:-$HOME/.openclaw}\\\"\\n\\n    export XDG_CONFIG_HOME=\\\"$STATE_DIR/agents/main/qmd/xdg-config\\\"\\n    export XDG_CACHE_HOME=\\\"$STATE_DIR/agents/main/qmd/xdg-cache\\\"\\n\\n    # (Optional) force an index refresh + embeddings\\n    qmd update\\n    qmd embed\\n\\n    # Warm up / trigger first-time model downloads\\n    qmd query \\\"test\\\" -c memory-root --json >/dev/null 2>&1\\n    ```\\n\\n**Config surface (`memory.qmd.*`)**\\n\\n* `command` (default `qmd`): override the executable path.\\n* characters\nCHUNK_OVERLAP = 200\n\nINDEX_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n\ndef read_file(filepath):\n    \"\"\"Read a markdown file and extract content.\"\"\"\n    with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n        content = f.read()\n    \n    # Extract frontmatter\n    if content.startswith('---'):\n        parts = content.split('---', 2)\n        if len(parts) >= 3:\n            frontmatter = parts[1]\n            body = parts[2].strip()\n            \n            # Parse frontmatter\n            meta = {}\n            for line in frontmatter.split('\\n'):\n                if ':' in line:\n                    key, val = line.split(':', 1)\n                    meta[key.strip().strip('\"')] = val.strip().strip('\"')\n            \n            return body, meta\n    \n    return content, {}\n\ndef chunk_text(text, source_file, meta):\n    \"\"\"Split text into overlapping chunks.\"\"\"\n    chunks = []\n    \n    # Split by paragraphs first\n    paragraphs = text.split('\\n\\n')\n    current_chunk = \"\"\n    chunk_id = 0\n    \n    for para in paragraphs:\n        para = para.strip()\n        if not para:\n            continue\n            \n        if len(current_chunk) + len(para) + 2 <= CHUNK_SIZE:\n            current_chunk += para + \"\\n\\n\"\n        else:\n            if current_chunk:\n                chunk_hash = hashlib.md5(f\"{source_file}:{chunk_id}\".encode()).hexdigest()[:12]\n                chunks.append({\n                    \"id\": f\"{source_file.replace('.md', '')}-{chunk_id}\",\n                    \"hash\": chunk_hash,\n                    \"text\": current_chunk.strip(),\n                    \"source\": source_file,\n                    \"title\": meta.get('title', source_file),\n                    \"url\": meta.get('source', ''),\n                    \"char_count\": len(current_chunk)\n                })\n                chunk_id += 1\n            \n            # Start new chunk with overlap\n            if CHUNK_OVERLAP > 0 and current_chunk:\n                overlap_text = current_chunk[-CHUNK_OVERLAP:]\n                current_chunk = overlap_text + para + \"\\n\\n\"\n            else:\n                current_chunk = para + \"\\n\\n\"\n    \n    # Don't forget the last chunk\n    if current_chunk.strip():\n        chunk_hash = hashlib.md5(f\"{source_file}:{chunk_id}\".encode()).hexdigest()[:12]\n        chunks.append({\n            \"id\": f\"{source_file.replace('.md', '')}-{chunk_id}\",\n            \"hash\": chunk_hash,\n            \"text\": current_chunk.strip(),\n            \"source\": source_file,\n            \"title\": meta.get('title', source_file),\n            \"url\": meta.get('source', ''),\n            \"char_count\": len(current_chunk)\n        })\n    \n    return chunks\n\ndef main():\n    \"\"\"Build the index.\"\"\"\n    print(f\"Building index from: {CLEAN_MD_DIR}\")\n    print(f\"Chunk size: {CHUNK_SIZE} chars, overlap: {CHUNK_OVERLAP}\")\n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n    \n    all_chunks = []\n    file_count = 0\n    \n    # Process each markdown file\n    for md_file in sorted(CLEAN_MD_DIR.glob(\"*.md\")):\n        file_count += 1\n        print(f\"Processing: {md_file.name}\")\n        \n        content, meta = read_file(md_file)\n        chunks = chunk_text(content, md_file.name, meta)\n        all_chunks.extend(chunks)\n        print(f\"  → {len(chunks)} chunks\")\n    \n    print(\"-\" * 50)\n    print(f\"Total files: {file_count}\")\n    print(f\"Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}\")\n    \n    # Save chunks\n    chunks_file = INDEX_DIR / \"chunks.jsonl\"\n    with open(chunks_file, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n        for chunk in all_chunks:\n            f.write(json.dumps(chunk, ensure_ascii=False) + '\\n')\n    \n    print(f\"Saved: {chunks_file}\")\n    \n    # Create metadata file\n    meta = {\n        \"corpus\": \"AbroadWorks Blog\",\n        \"source\": \"https://www.abroadworks.com/blog\",\n        \"created\": time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'),\n        \"file_count\": file_count,\n        \"chunk_count\": len(all_chunks),\n        \"chunk_size\": CHUNK_SIZE,\n        \"chunk_overlap\": CHUNK_OVERLAP,\n    }\n    \n    meta_file = INDEX_DIR / \"meta.json\"\n    with open(meta_file, 'w') as f:\n        json.dump(meta, f, indent=2)\n    \n    print(f\"Saved: {meta_file}\")\n    print(\"Done!\")\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()"}}],"stopReason":"toolUse","api":"ollama","provider":"ollama","model":"minimax-m2.5:cloud","usage":{"input":40293,"output":1224,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":41517,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}},"timestamp":1773929784909}}

The files are the\\nsource of truth; the model only \\\"remembers\\\" what gets written to disk.\\n\\nMemory search tools are provided by the active memory plugin (default:\\n`memory-core`). Disable memory plugins with `plugins.slots.memory = \\\"none\\\"`.\\n\\n## Memory files (Markdown)\\n\\nThe default workspace layout uses two memory layers:\\n\\n* `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`\\n  * Daily log (append-only).\\n  * Read today + yesterday at session start.\\n* `MEMORY.md` (optional)\\n  * Curated long-term memory.\\n  * If both `MEMORY.md` and `memory.md` exist at the workspace root, OpenClaw only loads `MEMORY.md`.\\n  * Lowercase `memory.md` is only used as a fallback when `MEMORY.md` is absent.\\n  * **Only load in the main, private session** (never in group contexts).\\n\\nThese files live under the workspace (`agents.defaults.workspace`, default\\n`~/.openclaw/workspace`). See [Agent workspace](/concepts/agent-workspace) for the full layout.\\n\\n## Memory tools\\n\\nOpenClaw exposes two agent-facing tools for these Markdown files:\\n\\n* `memory_search` — semantic recall over indexed snippets.\\n* `memory_get` — targeted read of a specific Markdown file/line range.\\n\\n`memory_get` now **degrades gracefully when a file doesn't exist** (for example,\\ntoday's daily log before the first write). Both the builtin manager and the QMD\\nbackend return `{ text: \\\"\\\", path }` instead of throwing `ENOENT`, so agents can\\nhandle \\\"nothing recorded yet\\\" and continue their workflow without wrapping the\\ntool call in try/catch logic.\\n\\n## When to write memory\\n\\n* Decisions, preferences, and durable facts go to `MEMORY.md`.\\n* Day-to-day notes and running context go to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`.\\n* If someone says \\\"remember this,\\\" write it down (do not keep it in RAM).\\n* This area is still evolving. It helps to remind the model to store memories; it will know what to do.\\n* If you want something to stick, **ask the bot to write it** into memory.\\n\\n## Automatic memory flush (pre-compaction ping)\\n\\nWhen a session is **close to auto-compaction**, OpenClaw triggers a **silent,\\nagentic turn** that reminds the model to write durable memory **before** the\\ncontext is compacted. The default prompts explicitly say the model *may reply*,\\nbut usually `NO_REPLY` is the correct response so the user never sees this turn.\\n\\nThis is controlled by `agents.defaults.compaction.memoryFlush`:\\n\\n```json5  theme={\\\"theme\\\":{\\\"light\\\":\\\"min-light\\\",\\\"dark\\\":\\\"min-dark\\\"}}\\n{\\n  agents: {\\n    defaults: {\\n      compaction: {\\n        reserveTokensFloor: 20000,\\n        memoryFlush: {\\n          enabled: true,\\n          softThresholdTokens: 4000,\\n          systemPrompt: \\\"Session nearing compaction. Store durable memories now.\\\",\\n          prompt: \\\"Write any lasting notes to memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md; reply with NO_REPLY if nothing to store.\\\",\\n        },\\n      },\\n    },\\n  },\\n}\\n```\\n\\nDetails:\\n\\n* **Soft threshold**: flush triggers when the session token estimate crosses\\n  `contextWindow - reserveTokensFloor - softThresholdTokens`.\\n* **Silent** by default: prompts include `NO_REPLY` so nothing is delivered.\\n* **Two prompts**: a user prompt plus a system prompt append the reminder.\\n* **One flush per compaction cycle** (tracked in `sessions.json`).\\n* **Workspace must be writable**: if the session runs sandboxed with\\n  `workspaceAccess: \\\"ro\\\"` or `\\\"none\\\"`, the flush is skipped.\\n\\nFor the full compaction lifecycle, see\\n[Session management + compaction](/reference/session-management-compaction).\\n\\n## Vector memory search\\n\\nOpenClaw can build a small vector index over `MEMORY.md` and `memory/*.md` so\\nsemantic queries can find related notes even when wording differs.\\n\\nDefaults:\\n\\n* Enabled by default.\\n* Watches memory files for changes (debounced).\\n* Configure memory search under `agents.defaults.memorySearch` (not top-level\\n  `memorySearch`).\\n* Uses remote embeddings by default. If `memorySearch.provider` is not set, OpenClaw auto-selects:\\n  1. `local` if a `memorySearch.local.modelPath` is configured and the file exists.\\n  2. `openai` if an OpenAI key can be resolved.\\n  3. `gemini` if a Gemini key can be resolved.\\n  4. `voyage` if a Voyage key can be resolved.\\n  5. `mistral` if a Mistral key can be resolved.\\n  6. Otherwise memory search stays disabled until configured.\\n* Local mode uses node-llama-cpp and may require `pnpm approve-builds`.\\n* Uses sqlite-vec (when available) to accelerate vector search inside SQLite.\\n* `memorySearch.provider = \\\"ollama\\\"` is also supported for local/self-hosted\\n  Ollama embeddings (`/api/embeddings`), but it is not auto-selected.\\n\\nRemote embeddings **require** an API key for the embedding provider. OpenClaw\\nresolves keys from auth profiles, `models.providers.*.apiKey`, or environment\\nvariables. Codex OAuth only covers chat/completions and does **not** satisfy\\nembeddings for memory search. For Gemini, use `GEMINI_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.google.apiKey`. For Voyage, use `VOYAGE_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.voyage.apiKey`. For Mistral, use `MISTRAL_API_KEY` or\\n`models.providers.mistral.apiKey`. Ollama typically does not require a real API\\nkey (a placeholder like `OLLAMA_API_KEY=ollama-local` is enough when needed by\\nlocal policy).\\nWhen using a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint,\\nset `memorySearch.remote.apiKey` (and optional `memorySearch.remote.headers`).\\n\\n### QMD backend (experimental)\\n\\nSet `memory.backend = \\\"qmd\\\"` to swap the built-in SQLite indexer for\\n[QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd): a local-first search sidecar that combines\\nBM25 + vectors + reranking. Markdown stays the source of truth; OpenClaw shells\\nout to QMD for retrieval. Key points:\\n\\n**Prereqs**\\n\\n* Disabled by default. Opt in per-config (`memory.backend = \\\"qmd\\\"`).\\n* Install the QMD CLI separately (`bun install -g https://github.com/tobi/qmd` or grab\\n  a release) and make sure the `qmd` binary is on the gateway’s `PATH`.\\n* QMD needs an SQLite build that allows extensions (`brew install sqlite` on\\n  macOS).\\n* QMD runs fully locally via Bun + `node-llama-cpp` and auto-downloads GGUF\\n  models from HuggingFace on first use (no separate Ollama daemon required).\\n* The gateway runs QMD in a self-contained XDG home under\\n  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/qmd/` by setting `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and\\n  `XDG_CACHE_HOME`.\\n* OS support: macOS and Linux work out of the box once Bun + SQLite are\\n  installed. Windows is best supported via WSL2.\\n\\n**How the sidecar runs**\\n\\n* The gateway writes a self-contained QMD home under\\n  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/qmd/` (config + cache + sqlite DB).\\n* Collections are created via `qmd collection add` from `memory.qmd.paths`\\n  (plus default workspace memory files), then `qmd update` + `qmd embed` run\\n  on boot and on a configurable interval (`memory.qmd.update.interval`,\\n  default 5 m).\\n* The gateway now initializes the QMD manager on startup, so periodic update\\n  timers are armed even before the first `memory_search` call.\\n* Boot refresh now runs in the background by default so chat startup is not\\n  blocked; set `memory.qmd.update.waitForBootSync = true` to keep the previous\\n  blocking behavior.\\n* Searches run via `memory.qmd.searchMode` (default `qmd search --json`; also\\n  supports `vsearch` and `query`). If the selected mode rejects flags on your\\n  QMD build, OpenClaw retries with `qmd query`. If QMD fails or the binary is\\n  missing, OpenClaw automatically falls back to the builtin SQLite manager so\\n  memory tools keep working.\\n* OpenClaw does not expose QMD embed batch-size tuning today; batch behavior is\\n  controlled by QMD itself.\\n* **First search may be slow**: QMD may download local GGUF models (reranker/query\\n  expansion) on the first `qmd query` run.\\n  * OpenClaw sets `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`/`XDG_CACHE_HOME` automatically when it runs QMD.\\n  * If you want to pre-download models manually (and warm the same index OpenClaw\\n    uses), run a one-off query with the agent’s XDG dirs.\\n\\n    OpenClaw’s QMD state lives under your **state dir** (defaults to `~/.openclaw`).\\n    You can point `qmd` at the exact same index by exporting the same XDG vars\\n    OpenClaw uses:\\n\\n    ```bash  theme={\\\"theme\\\":{\\\"light\\\":\\\"min-light\\\",\\\"dark\\\":\\\"min-dark\\\"}}\\n    # Pick the same state dir OpenClaw uses\\n    STATE_DIR=\\\"${OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR:-$HOME/.openclaw}\\\"\\n\\n    export XDG_CONFIG_HOME=\\\"$STATE_DIR/agents/main/qmd/xdg-config\\\"\\n    export XDG_CACHE_HOME=\\\"$STATE_DIR/agents/main/qmd/xdg-cache\\\"\\n\\n    # (Optional) force an index refresh + embeddings\\n    qmd update\\n    qmd embed\\n\\n    # Warm up / trigger first-time model downloads\\n    qmd query \\\"test\\\" -c memory-root --json >/dev/null 2>&1\\n    ```\\n\\n**Config surface (`memory.qmd.*`)**\\n\\n* `command` (default `qmd`): override the executable path.\\n* `searchMode` (default `search`): pick which QMD command backs\\n  `memory_search` (`search`, `vsearch`, `query`).\\n* `includeDefaultMemory` (default `true`): auto-index `MEMORY.md` + `memory/**/*.md`.\\n* `paths[]`: add extra directories/files (`path`, optional `pattern`, optional\\n  stable `name`).\\n* `sessions`: opt into session JSONL indexing (`enabled`, `retentionDays`,\\n arguments.command:
cat << 'PYEOF' > /tmp/audit2.py
import json
import re
from pathlib import Path

SESSION_DIR = Path.home() / ".openclaw/agents/main/sessions"

# Find sessions modified between Mar 17-27
window_sessions = []
for f in SESSION_DIR.glob("*.jsonl"):
    mtime = f.stat().st_mtime
    if 1773696000 <= mtime < 1774809600:
        window_sessions.append(f)

note_patterns = [
    r'\bstop\b.*\bnot\b',  # skip common false positives
]

# More targeted patterns - must appear as complete phrases
strict_patterns = [
    r'\btake\s+notes\b',
    r'\bmake\s+a\s+note\b',
    r'\bI\s+want\s+you\s+to\s+remember\b',
    r'\bnote\s+this\b',
    r'\bdon'"'"'t\s+forget\b',
    r'\bsave.*to.*file',
    r'\bwrite.*down\b',
    r'\blog\s+this\b',
    r'\bappend\s+this\b',
    r'\badd\s+this\s+to\b',
    r'\bremember\s+that\b',
    r'\bI\s+am\s+noting\b',
    r'\bPlease\s+note\b',
    r'\bit\s+was\b.*\bremember\b',
    r'\bput\s+this\s+in\b',
]

for sess_file in sorted(window_sessions):
    matches = []
    try:
        with open(sess_file) as f:
            for line in f:
                try:
                    obj = json.loads(line)
                    if obj.get("type") != "message":
                        continue
                    msg = obj.get("message", {})
                    if msg.get("role") != "user":
                        continue
                    ts = obj.get("timestamp", "")
                    content = msg.get("content", [])
                    for block in content:
                        if block.get("type") == "text":
                            text = block["text"]
                            # Skip cron/system messages
                            if "cron:" in text or "[Subagent Context]" in text:
                                continue
                            if "HEARTBEAT.md" in text or "Pre-compaction" in text:
                                continue
                            # Must be user text (has GMT+2 style timestamp or clear user message)
                            for pat in strict_patterns:
                                if re.search(pat, text, re.I):
                                    matches.append((ts, text[:300]))
                except:
                    pass
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error with {sess_file.name}: {e}")
        continue
    
    if matches:
        print(f"\n=== {sess_file.name} ===")
        for ts, text in matches:
            print(f"  [{ts}]")
            print(f"    → {text[:200]}")
            print()

cat << 'PYEOF' > /tmp/audit3.py
import json
import re
from pathlib import Path

SESSION_DIR = Path.home() / ".openclaw/agents/main/sessions"

# Note instruction patterns - things Tiny would say
NOTE_PATTERNS = [
    r'\btake\s+notes\b',
    r'\bmake\s+(?:a\s+)?note\b',
    r'\bI\s+want\s+you\s+to\s+remember\b',
    r'\bnote\s+(?:this|that|any)\b',
    r"\bdon't\s+forget\b",
    r'\bsave.*to\b',
    r'\bwrite.*down\b',
    r'\blog\s+(?:this|it)\b',
    r'\badd.*to\s+(?:memory|notes?)\b',
    r'\bremember\s+(?:that|to)\b',
    r'\bput\s+this\s+in\b',
    r'\brecord\s+this\b',
    r'\bkeep\s+(?:a\s+)?note\b',
    r'\bkeep\s+track\b',
    r'\bnote\s+for\s+later\b',
    r'\bremember.*for\s+me\b',
    r'\bI\s+(?:am\s+)?noting\b',
    r'\bnote\s+the\b',
    r'\bPlease\s+note\b',
    r'\bflag\b.*\bfor\s+later\b',
]

window_sessions = []
for f in SESSION_DIR.glob("*.jsonl"):
    mtime = f.stat().st_mtime
    if 1773696000 <= mtime < 1774809600:
        window_sessions.append(f)

all_findings = []

for sess_file in sorted(window_sessions):
    try:
        with open(sess_file) as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
        
        for line_idx, line in enumerate(lines):
            try:
                obj = json.loads(line)
            except:
                continue
            
            if obj.get("type") != "message":
                continue
            
            msg = obj.get("message", {})
            if msg.get("role") != "user":
                continue
            
            ts = obj.get("timestamp", "")
            
            # Check each text block
            content = msg.get("content", [])
            for block in content:
                if block.get("type") != "text":
                    continue
                text = block["text"]
                
                # Skip system/metadata messages
                if "Sender (untrusted" in text:
                    continue
                if "[Subagent Context]" in text:
                    continue
                if "cron:" in text[:50]:
                    continue
                if text.strip().startswith("{"):
                    continue
                if "HEARTBEAT" in text:
                    continue
                
                for pat in NOTE_PATTERNS:
                    if re.search(pat, text, re.I):
                        # Extract relevant snippet (clean)
                        snippet = text.strip()[:400]
                        all_findings.append({
                            'session': sess_file.name,
                            'timestamp': ts,
                            'snippet': snippet,
                            'pattern_matched': pat
                        })
                        break
    except Exception as e:
        pass

print(f"Total note instruction findings: {len(all_findings)}")
print()
for f in all_findings:
    print(f"=== {f['session']} [{f['timestamp']}] ===")
    print(f"  Pattern: {f['pattern_matched']}")
    print(f"  → {f['snippet'][:250]}")
    print()
